sentenceStructure.openingStyle: Conversation quote or product reveal opener
sentenceStructure.paragraphPattern: Short paragraphs (1-4 sentences), single-sentence paragraphs for emphasis, frequent line breaks

avoidList: at the end of the day
signaturePhrases: made me have to take a walk
This is exactly why we built
No tricks, no toys, just rapid shipping and results
puts the math in your favor
hiring the playbook, not the brain
Most teams aren't built for this

rhetoric.hookPattern: Conversation quote opener or product reveal
rhetoric.ctaStyle: Direct engagement ask (comment keyword / DM me) or bold closing statement about the future

canonicalExamples.text: A CMO told me yesterday: "We spent $47K in AI marketing tool licenses and not only isn't performance better, we feel even more bloated than ever with extra work."
canonicalExamples.why: Conversation opener with specific dollar amount and direct quote - his highest-engagement pattern

canonicalExamples.text: AI just reminded us of something brutal: The ground can shift overnight.
canonicalExamples.why: Breaking news hook with signature phrase - bold, urgent, concise

canonicalExamples.text:
I just spoke to technical founder of a series B startup, and he told me something I hear >50% of the time with this ICP:

He'd tried ~12 marketers, all failed. "Marketing is our weakest link."

Most felt like "hiring the playbook, not the brain." Too manual. Too slow. Hard to measure.

Then he contrasted it with his software engineers:
Engineers build their own tools.
They measure output precisely.
They justify headcount by shipping product.
More engineers = more output + value.

That's what he expected marketing to deliver, especially in the age of "growth".

When I explained what our AI Marketers do, he finally heard what he wanted.

- Combines marketing instinct with technical ability
- Automates and rebuilds workflows, not just runs them
- Thinks like a builder, not a playbook follower
- Measured like technical product

His response? "Sign me up."
Marketing doesn't need more playbooks or theory.
It needs to evolve the job the way software engineers work - build, ship, measure.
canonicalExamples.why: Third highest engagement - conversation opener, contrast structure, bullet list, bold closing statement. Exemplifies all core patterns.

canonicalExamples.text: If you want to hire/speak to/learn from one of our AI CMOs - just comment "AI CMO"
canonicalExamples.why: Direct engagement ask with specific keyword action - his most effective CTA pattern

canonicalExamples.text:
AI tools are commodities now.

AI-native marketers who know how to wield them? That's the competitive advantage now.
canonicalExamples.why: Contrast closing with signature vocabulary - tools vs talent, commodity vs advantage

canonicalExamples.text:
"Growth Marketers" optimized channels.
"AI Marketers" rebuild the workflows themselves. That's the difference.

Lifecycle: Not just sending campaigns, but designing AI-powered journeys that write, send, and optimize in real time.

Paid: Not just testing ads manually, but building creative engines that experiment at 10x the pace.

Analytics: Not just reading dashboards, but engineering AI systems that surface insights and trigger action automatically.

One role squeezes more juice out of the system.
The other redesigns the system entirely.

Growth was about optimization.
AI is about reinvention.

That's what makes an AI Marketer different.
canonicalExamples.why: Bold claim opener with parallel contrast structure throughout - defines the AI Marketer category

openingPatterns.pattern: Direct quote or observation from a real conversation with a CMO, founder, or marketing leader, grounding the post immediately in a specific interaction.
examples: A CMO told me yesterday: "We spent $47K in AI marketing tool licenses and not only isn't performance better, we feel even more bloated than ever with extra work."
I just spoke to technical founder of a series B startup, and he told me something I hear >50% of the time with this ICP:
Talked with two CMOs today doing $10m and $100m+ in revenue at their companies.
Another conversation with an AI-Native CMO blew my socks off:

openingPatterns.pattern: Genuine excitement about something MH-1 or the team has built. Always starts with "I just saw" and includes an emotional reaction.
examples: I just saw the latest version of our LLM Visibility Module that we're rolling out to MH-1 customers.
I just saw our new MH-1 ad creative testing module generate dozens of ad variations in the time it used to take our teams to brief one campaign.
Our MH-1 AI marketing solution just shipped an automation module for a customer that made me have to take a walk.

openingPatterns.pattern: Responds to industry developments with immediate relevance. Names the event, gives an emotional reaction, then explains implications.
examples: Gemini 3 dropped yesterday. Everyone's excited. 1000 posts just published telling everyone what they built to date is hot garbage.
AI just reminded us of something brutal: The ground can shift overnight.
SEO vs GEO has been one of the most volatile areas of demand and performance we've seen... maybe ever.

openingPatterns.pattern: Names a recurring pattern or challenge observed across multiple companies or conversations.
examples: Marketing leaders are getting stuck in the same place when it comes to AI transformation.
As marketing orgs try to adopt AI, a new pattern has emerged from companies we are working with. Indecision.
Marketing is in a constant state of chaos.

openingPatterns.pattern: Definitive statement or quote that demands attention. Short, declarative, no hedging.
examples: "Growth Marketers" optimized channels. "AI Marketers" rebuild the workflows themselves. That's the difference.
We've been patient with our AI-related offering. We knew the vetting bar had to be higher than any role we've ever had.

openingPatterns.pattern: Leads with specific quantitative findings from MarketerHire data or industry research.
examples: ~80% of CMOs we polled said they know they need a plan for the age of AI Marketing. Almost all admit they don't have one yet or are low in confidence in the one they do.
One emerging trendline we're seeing among B2B CMOs on MarketerHire: a shift in where new marketing investment is going in 2025.

placementRules: Extremely rare - only occasional thinking face emoji
Never uses emoji strings or emoji for emphasis
No emoji in openers or closers as a pattern
Single emoji (if any) as standalone line after a thought-provoking point

structuralPatterns.name: Conversation -> Insight -> Product Bridge -> CTA
structure: Real conversation opener with specific role and context
Key insights from the conversation (quoted or paraphrased)
Why this matters / what it reveals about the market
Natural bridge to MH-1 or MarketerHire capability
Direct CTA (comment keyword or DM)
structuralPatterns.useCase: CMO conversation posts, customer pain point posts

structuralPatterns.name: Product Reveal -> Problem -> Feature List -> Results -> Multiplier Close
structure: I just saw opener with emotional reaction
The problem this solves (velocity/efficiency framing)
Dash-prefixed bullet list of capabilities
Early results with specific metrics
It doesn't replace... it multiplies framing
CTA for access
structuralPatterns.useCase: Product capability and feature announcement posts

structuralPatterns.name: Breaking News -> Impact -> Implications -> Most Teams Aren't Built -> Competitive Advantage
structure: Industry event or model release
What changed and immediate impact
Bulleted implications for marketing leaders
Most teams aren't built for this
What winning teams do differently
Bold closing statement about competitive advantage

structuralPatterns.name: Pattern Identification -> Elaboration -> Catch-22s -> Solution -> Bold Close
structure: Name the pattern or problem (single dramatic word on its own line)
What teams are experiencing
The catch-22s or blockers preventing resolution
What winning teams do differently
Bold imperative closing statement
structuralPatterns.useCase: Problem pattern and thought leadership posts

structuralPatterns.name: Bold Claim -> Parallel Contrast -> Era Statement
structure: Definitive statement setting up old vs new
Parallel Not just X, but Y structures across multiple domains
Single-sentence contrast lines
Era-level framing: Growth was about X. AI is about Y.
structuralPatterns.useCase: Category definition and role differentiation posts

structuralPatterns.name: Historical Arc -> We're At That Moment -> Future Statement
structure: Data or trend opener
Historical parallel (digital marketing era -> growth marketing era)
We're at that same moment now
Era progression: Digital -> Growth -> AI
Bold optimistic closing about the opportunity
structuralPatterns.useCase: AI marketing era and transformation narrative posts

neverUse: Vague claims without data or conversation grounding
at the end of the day
formattingToAvoid: Hashtags - never used in any post
Excessive emojis - extremely rare, occasional thinking face only
Long paragraphs without breaks - always break every 1-4 sentences
Headers or bold formatting within posts
Dot-prefixed bullet points - always uses dash-prefixed bullets
Over-formatted posts with bold, italics, or headers
toneToAvoid: Lecturing without grounding in conversations or data
Promoting MH-1 without providing value first
Dismissing AI concerns - always acknowledge them first
Positioning AI as replacement - always frame as multiplier

hashtagStyle.format: N/A - Chris never uses hashtags in any post

taggingStyle.frequency: Rare - not a significant pattern in his posts
patterns: Occasionally references MarketerHire or MH-1 by name
References roles (CMO, founder) rather than tagging specific people
